definition: The constitutional principle that legislative, executive, and judicial powers are divided among the three branches of government. In this context, it means that Congress — not the courts — decides what conduct to criminalize and how severely to punish it, so courts should not impose additional punishment without clear congressional authorization.
examples: Petitioner argues that courts must be careful before concluding Congress wanted to double-punish one crime, because setting criminal penalties is a legislative function committed to Congress by the Constitution.
